---
title: "Geocomputation with R"
subtitle: "Bridges to GIS software — qgisprocess"
author: "Jannes Muenchow, Jakub Nowosad"
date: 2026-07-06
---

## Bonus: bridges to GIS software {.section-divider}

::: {.callout-note}
This topic needs the **qgis** Docker image
(`ghcr.io/geocompx/qgis`), not `latest` — it ships QGIS + `qgisprocess`.
:::

## Why bridge R to a GIS?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
R is already a capable GIS — but dedicated GIS expose **hundreds of
geoalgorithms** R doesn't have:

::: {.incremental}
- terrain & hydrology (slope, hillshade, catchment area, wetness index)
- LiDAR / point-cloud processing
- specialized vector overlays
:::

R **originated as an interface language** [@bivand_applied_2013] — so it's a
natural front-end to that toolbox.
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
![](/img/r_qgis_puzzle.png)
:::
:::

## The R–GIS bridges

- **qgisprocess** → QGIS (successor to `RQGIS`) — our focus today
- **rgrass** → GRASS GIS (direct)
- **Rsagacmd** → SAGA (direct)

. . .

`qgis_process` is a **unified interface** to native QGIS, **GDAL**, and
**GRASS** (once enabled) — 300+ algorithms, 1000+ with providers.

. . .

Heads-up: the **SAGA provider was dropped from recent QGIS**, so SAGA is *not*
reachable via `qgisprocess` — use **Rsagacmd** directly for SAGA tools.

## Getting started

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(qgisprocess)
#> QGIS version: 3.44.11-Solothurn

# which providers/plugins are available?
qgis_plugins()

# GRASS ships disabled — enable it once
qgis_enable_plugins("grassprovider", quiet = TRUE)

qgis_providers() # gdal, grass, qgis, native, 3d + algorithm counts
```

## Find and inspect an algorithm

Let's compute a **topographic wetness index (TWI)** — *where does water
accumulate?* It's high in flat, valley-bottom cells with a large upslope
catchment and low on steep ridges (`TWI = ln(upslope area / tan(slope))`). A
classic terrain attribute for hydrology and landslide studies — *not* in base
`terra`.

```{r}
#| eval: false
qgis_search_algorithms("watershed")
#> provider  algorithm          algorithm_title
#> grass     grass:r.watershed  r.watershed

# what arguments and outputs does it have?
qgis_show_help("grass:r.watershed")
```

## Run it — from R, on an R object

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(terra)
elev <- rast(system.file("ex/elev.tif", package = "terra"))

res <- qgis_run_algorithm(
  "grass:r.watershed",
  elevation = elev, threshold = 100
)

# 'tci' = topographic convergence (wetness) index
twi <- qgis_as_terra(res$tci)
plot(twi, col = hcl.colors(50, "Blues", rev = TRUE))
```

## Result: topographic wetness index

![Wetness index (`grass:r.watershed`, `tci`) — high values trace the drainage network](/img/qgis_wetness.png){width="50%"}

`sf`/`terra` objects go in, results come back as `sf`/`terra` — the GIS is just
another function call in a reproducible R workflow.

## Recap

- **qgisprocess** turns 1000+ GIS geoalgorithms into ordinary R function calls.
- Inputs/outputs are `sf`/`terra` objects — fully scriptable and reproducible.
- Enable providers (e.g. `grassprovider`) to unlock GRASS algorithms.

<br>

Further reading:

- Geocomputation with R — [*Bridges to GIS software*](https://r.geocompx.org/gis)
- [`qgisprocess` documentation](https://r-spatial.github.io/qgisprocess/)

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
